Reserve Bank of India
Master Direction - Know Your Customer (KYC) Direction, 2016 (Updated as on February 24, 2023)
RBI/DBR/2015-16/18
DBR.AML.BC.No.81/14.01.001/2015-16
February 25, 2016 (Updated February 24, 2023)

To,
All Regulated Entities

Madam / Dear Sir,

Master Direction – Know Your Customer (KYC) Direction, 2016

CHAPTER I – PRELIMINARY

1. Short Title and Commencement
These Directions shall be called the Reserve Bank of India (Know Your Customer (KYC)) Directions, 2016 and shall come into force from the date of their issue.

2. Applicability
These Directions apply to all Regulated Entities (REs) including Scheduled Commercial Banks, Regional Rural Banks, Local Area Banks, All-India Financial Institutions, Non-Banking Financial Companies, Cooperative Banks, Payment Banks, and Small Finance Banks.

CHAPTER II – CUSTOMER DUE DILIGENCE

3. Customer Identification and Verification
(a) Regulated Entities shall carry out Customer Due Diligence (CDD) measures while establishing a business relationship. CDD shall include:
    (i) Identifying and verifying the customer using reliable, independent source documents, data, or information;
    (ii) Identifying the beneficial owner and taking reasonable measures to verify the identity of the beneficial owner;
    (iii) Understanding and, as appropriate, obtaining information on the purpose and intended nature of the business relationship.
(b) All existing KYC records of customers shall be updated periodically. High-risk customers shall be subjected to enhanced due diligence and their KYC records shall be updated at least every 2 years. Medium-risk customers every 8 years, and low-risk customers every 10 years.
(c) Customer identification documents shall be verified through Video Based Customer Identification Process (V-CIP) or through the Aadhaar OTP based e-KYC method as prescribed by UIDAI.

4. Risk Categorisation of Customers
(a) Regulated Entities shall put in place a Board-approved policy on risk categorisation of customers. The policy shall define criteria for categorisation of customers into Low, Medium, and High risk categories.
(b) Regulated Entities shall assign risk ratings to customers at the time of onboarding and review these ratings at periodic intervals, based on transaction behaviour, geographical risk, and customer background.
(c) Customers who are Politically Exposed Persons (PEPs), their family members, or close associates shall be mandatorily classified as High-risk customers.

5. Enhanced Due Diligence (EDD)
(a) Enhanced Due Diligence shall be applied to customers posing higher risk of money laundering or terrorist financing. EDD measures shall include:
    (i) Obtaining additional identification documents;
    (ii) Visiting the place of business or residence of the customer;
    (iii) Obtaining senior management approval before establishing or continuing the business relationship.
(b) Regulated Entities shall conduct enhanced scrutiny of all transactions involving High-risk customers and maintain transaction records with complete audit trails.

CHAPTER III – ANTI-MONEY LAUNDERING (AML) AND COUNTER-FINANCING OF TERRORISM (CFT)

6. Suspicious Transaction Reporting
(a) All Regulated Entities shall file Suspicious Transaction Reports (STRs) with the Financial Intelligence Unit – India (FIU-IND) within 7 working days of forming a suspicion about a transaction.
(b) Regulated Entities shall designate a Principal Officer responsible for STR filing. The contact details of the Principal Officer shall be reported to RBI and FIU-IND.
(c) The Compliance Officer shall maintain a register of all STRs filed and submit a quarterly summary report to the Board's Audit Committee.

7. Cash Transaction Reporting
(a) All cash transactions of Rs. 10 lakh and above, whether a single transaction or series of connected transactions within one month, shall be reported to FIU-IND as Cash Transaction Reports (CTRs).
(b) CTRs shall be filed by the 15th of the following month for the previous month's transactions. Failure to file within the prescribed period shall attract regulatory action.
(c) Regulated Entities shall maintain records of all CTRs and STRs for a period of 10 years.

8. AML/CFT Training
(a) Regulated Entities shall put in place an ongoing AML/CFT employee training programme. All frontline staff involved in customer onboarding and transaction processing shall mandatorily attend AML/CFT training at least once every calendar year.
(b) Training records shall be maintained and made available for regulatory inspection. New employees must complete AML/CFT induction training within 30 days of joining.
(c) The Chief Compliance Officer shall certify annually to the Board that all required training has been completed.

CHAPTER IV – RECORD KEEPING AND REPORTING

9. Record Maintenance
(a) Regulated Entities shall maintain all KYC records, account files, and business correspondence for a period of 10 years from the date of cessation of business relationship.
(b) KYC records shall be maintained in a manner that enables quick retrieval for regulatory inspection. Records shall be available in digital format with search capability.
(c) Regulated Entities shall implement a centralised KYC records management system that allows cross-branch and cross-product access to KYC data.

10. Regulatory Reporting to RBI
(a) Regulated Entities shall submit a quarterly compliance report on KYC/AML/CFT to RBI covering: number of accounts opened, risk categorisation summary, number of STRs and CTRs filed, training completion status, and KYC updation status.
(b) The compliance report shall be submitted within 21 days of the end of each quarter. The report shall be certified by the Chief Compliance Officer.
(c) Any material deficiency in KYC/AML/CFT compliance identified during internal audit shall be reported to RBI within 15 days of the audit report.

CHAPTER V – GOVERNANCE

11. Board-Level Oversight
(a) The Board of Directors shall approve a comprehensive KYC/AML/CFT policy, which shall be reviewed at least annually and updated to incorporate regulatory changes.
(b) The Board's Audit Committee shall receive a quarterly compliance report from the Chief Compliance Officer on KYC/AML/CFT matters.
(c) Regulated Entities shall designate a senior management level officer as the Chief Compliance Officer with specific responsibility for KYC/AML/CFT oversight.

These Directions shall be read with the Prevention of Money-Laundering Act, 2002 and the Prevention of Money-Laundering (Maintenance of Records) Rules, 2005.

Yours faithfully,

(A. K. Misra)
Principal Chief General Manager
